hero.tagline: Robots that fly - multirotor, fixed-wing, and the autopilots that fly them
hero.intro: Aerial robots add a third spatial dimension and a constant fight against gravity. The hardest parts of grounded robotics - balance, stability, fail-safes - are foreground concerns from the first power-up. This branch covers the modern drone stack: how a multirotor stays in the air (motor mixing, cascade attitude + position control), the production autopilots (PX4, Ardupilot, Betaflight), the MAVLink protocol every drone speaks, autonomous mission planning, swarms, and real-world applications from agriculture to inspection to defense.

sections.heading: Drone types - multirotor, fixed-wing, VTOL
sections.body: Three families of aerial vehicles with very different physics and use cases. Multirotors (quad/hexa/octa) hover, fly slow, dead-stop on a dime - and burn battery doing it. Fixed-wing aircraft glide efficiently over long distances but need a runway and constant forward motion. VTOL hybrids (tilt-rotor, tilt-wing, lift+cruise) take off vertically then transition to wing-borne flight - best of both, mechanically complex.
bullets: Quadrotor - 4 motors, hover, ~25 min flight time on 5000 mAh battery, the default class
Hexa / octa - redundancy (one motor fails → still flies), heavier payload
Fixed-wing - 60+ min flight, 100+ km range, but needs a runway and ~10 m/s minimum airspeed
VTOL hybrids - vertical takeoff + cruise efficiency; military and delivery (Wisk, Joby, Zipline)
Sizes: tinywhoop (~25g) → cinematic (~2 kg) → industrial (~20 kg) → eVTOL (~1000 kg)
Pick by mission: hover/inspect → multirotor; survey miles → fixed-wing; both → VTOL

sections.heading: Multirotor dynamics + motor mixing
sections.body: A quadrotor has 4 propellers that each produce a thrust force and a torque (from their drag). Two diagonally-opposite propellers spin one direction, the other two spin the opposite - yaw torques cancel in hover. Roll is induced by spinning the right pair faster than the left; pitch by spinning the rear faster than the front; yaw by spinning one diagonal pair faster than the other. The 'motor mixer' translates desired (thrust, roll, pitch, yaw) commands into per-motor RPM.

codeBlocks.title: Quadrotor X-configuration motor mixer
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

# Mixer: rows = motors (FL, FR, RR, RL), cols = (T, roll, pitch, yaw)
# X-frame: FL spins CCW, FR CW, RR CCW, RL CW
MIXER = np.array([
    [1, -1, +1, +1],   # FL
    [1, +1, +1, -1],   # FR
    [1, +1, -1, +1],   # RR
    [1, -1, -1, -1]    # RL
])

def mix(T, roll, pitch, yaw):
    """Returns 4 normalized motor commands in [0, 1]."""
    cmd = MIXER @ np.array([T, roll, pitch, yaw])
    cmd = (cmd - cmd.min()) / (cmd.max() - cmd.min() + 1e-9)   # normalize
    return np.clip(cmd, 0, 1)

bullets: Thrust scales with rotor speed²; for hover at mass m: each motor produces m·g/4
Hover efficiency: ~5-7 grams of thrust per Watt; bigger props are dramatically more efficient
Yaw authority is weak (drag-derived) - quadrotors spin slowly in yaw, hexas worse
Centre of gravity matters - off-CG = constant trim correction = wasted flight time

sections.heading: Attitude control - the inner loop
sections.body: A multirotor is unstable; the attitude controller runs at 500-1000 Hz on the flight controller's microcontroller and is what keeps it in the air. The standard architecture is cascaded PID: an outer attitude loop (target roll/pitch/yaw) outputs target body rates; an inner rate loop (target body rates from gyro) outputs torque. The mixer turns torque into per-motor commands. Tuning is mostly the inner rate loop - get it stable and the outer loop falls into place.
bullets: Inner loop: rate controller. Input: gyro. Output: motor commands. ~500-1000 Hz
Outer loop: attitude controller. Input: orientation estimate. Output: rate setpoint. ~250-500 Hz
Tuning order: P → D → I, on roll → pitch → yaw, then test with gentle inputs
Pid → MPC: production drones (Skydio, DJI Pro) replace inner PID with model predictive control
Quaternion attitude controller - avoids singularities the Euler version has
INDI (Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion) - modern alternative, robust to model error

sections.heading: Position control - the outer loop
sections.body: On top of the attitude / rate cascade sits the position controller. Input: target (x, y, z) in world frame + estimated current position from GPS/VIO/optical-flow. Output: target acceleration → target attitude. A typical PX4 multirotor uses a P controller for position → P+D for velocity → conversion to thrust + tilt → attitude setpoint. GPS-denied flight (indoor, under bridges) needs an alternative position source - usually optical flow + lidar altimeter, or VIO.
bullets: Position → velocity → acceleration → attitude - three nested loops
GPS hold: ±1 m typical with consumer GPS; ±5 cm with RTK base station
Indoor: downward optical flow + lidar/ToF altimeter (Bitcraze Crazyflie, PX4 indoor mode)
VIO (visual-inertial odometry): T265 (discontinued), Skydio's custom stack, OpenVINS
Wind compensation: integral term in velocity loop or feed-forward from estimated wind

sections.heading: Autopilots - PX4, Ardupilot, Crazyflie (and FPV's Betaflight)
sections.body: For autonomy work, two open-source stacks dominate: PX4 (BSD, ROS 2 native via the px4_ros_com bridge, used by Skydio / Auterion / Wing) and Ardupilot (GPL, more mature for fixed-wing / VTOL / boats / rovers, used by inspection + survey vendors). Crazyflie firmware powers Bitcraze's tiny indoor research drone, popular in academia for swarm research. Betaflight is FPV-racing only (rate mode, no autonomy, no waypoints) - mention it for completeness but it is not what you reach for if you're building a robot. The serious 2026 hardware is the Pixhawk 6X / 6C with an H7 SoC and dual IMU.
bullets: PX4 - BSD, Pixhawk hardware family, ROS 2 native, modern default for research + serious commercial drones
Ardupilot - GPL, supports more vehicle types, mature, used by inspection + survey vendors
Crazyflie firmware - tiny (~25 g) drone, Python API, perfect for swarm research
Betaflight - FPV racing only, rates not angles, no autonomy features. Skip for robotics
Hardware: Pixhawk 6X / 6C is the modern flight controller; H7 SoC, dual IMU

codeBlocks.title: PX4 SITL + ROS 2 bridge
codeBlocks.code:
# Build PX4 with SITL
cd ~/{{ws}}/src && git clone --recursive https://github.com/PX4/PX4-Autopilot.git
cd PX4-Autopilot && make px4_sitl gz_x500

# In another shell - bring up the ROS 2 bridge
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-px4-msgs ros-jazzy-px4-ros-com
ros2 launch px4_ros_com sensor_combined_listener.launch.py

sections.heading: MAVLink - the drone protocol
sections.body: MAVLink is the messaging protocol drones use to talk to ground control stations (QGroundControl, Mission Planner) and companion computers. Compact binary format, ~280 standard message types (HEARTBEAT, ATTITUDE, GLOBAL_POSITION_INT, COMMAND_LONG), runs over serial or UDP. Both PX4 and Ardupilot speak it natively; pymavlink is the canonical Python library for writing your own GCS or autonomous mission scripts.

codeBlocks.title: pymavlink - connect, arm, take off, land
codeBlocks.code:
from pymavlink import mavutil
import time

m = mavutil.mavlink_connection('udp:127.0.0.1:14550')
m.wait_heartbeat()

# Switch to GUIDED mode
m.mav.set_mode_send(m.target_system,
    mavutil.mavlink.MAV_MODE_FLAG_CUSTOM_MODE_ENABLED, 4)

# Arm
m.mav.command_long_send(m.target_system, m.target_component,
    mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_COMPONENT_ARM_DISARM, 0,
    1, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)

# Takeoff to 5 m
m.mav.command_long_send(m.target_system, m.target_component,
    mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_NAV_TAKEOFF, 0,
    0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 5)

time.sleep(20)
# Land
m.mav.command_long_send(m.target_system, m.target_component,
    mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_NAV_LAND, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0)

sections.heading: Autonomous flight - missions, geofence, fail-safes
sections.body: A 'mission' is an ordered list of waypoints + per-waypoint actions (take photo, drop payload, hold for N seconds). The autopilot executes the mission while the companion computer handles anything outside the autopilot's repertoire - vision, dynamic obstacle avoidance, decisions. Production drones run a layered fail-safe: GPS lost? Hover. Comms lost? Return to launch (RTL). Battery low? Land here. Geofence breach? Hard stop. Each fail-safe has its own threshold and action.
bullets: Waypoint missions: WAYPOINT, LOITER, JUMP, conditional commands; planned in QGC / Mission Planner
Geofence: polygonal allowed area; breach = automatic action (RTL, land, brake)
Fail-safes: GPS loss, RC loss, telemetry loss, battery low, geofence - each independent
RTL (return to launch): climb to safe altitude, fly to home, land
Companion computer: NVIDIA Jetson / Raspberry Pi running ROS 2 + perception + decision
Obstacle avoidance: Skydio's deep learning stack, PX4's collision_prevention with SF45/B lidar

codeBlocks.title: Upload a 4-waypoint mission via pymavlink
codeBlocks.code:
from pymavlink import mavutil

def upload_mission(m, waypoints):
    """waypoints = [(lat, lon, alt_m, hold_s), ...]"""
    m.mav.mission_clear_all_send(m.target_system, m.target_component)
    m.mav.mission_count_send(m.target_system, m.target_component, len(waypoints))

    for i, (lat, lon, alt, hold) in enumerate(waypoints):
        msg = m.recv_match(type='MISSION_REQUEST', blocking=True)
        m.mav.mission_item_send(
            m.target_system, m.target_component, i,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_FRAME_GLOBAL_RELATIVE_ALT,
            mavutil.mavlink.MAV_CMD_NAV_WAYPOINT,
            0, 1,                       # current, autocontinue
            hold, 5, 0, 0,              # hold time, accept radius, pass radius, yaw
            lat, lon, alt)
    m.recv_match(type='MISSION_ACK', blocking=True)

sections.heading: Aerial perception - what cameras + sensors to fly
sections.body: Drones can carry every sensor a ground robot can - but with strict weight + power budgets. The standard payload set: a downward camera + lidar altimeter for indoor positioning, a forward camera for FPV + obstacle detection, a gimbaled camera for the actual mission imagery (RGB / thermal / multispectral). Larger drones add side LiDAR for inspection, gas sensors for pipeline survey, hyperspectral for agriculture.
bullets: Downward optical flow (PX4Flow, Ascent Vision) - replaces GPS indoors
Lidar altimeter (TF-Mini, SF11) - accurate altitude, especially over varying terrain
Gimbal: 3-axis brushless gimbal stabilizes the imaging payload to ~0.01° accuracy
Thermal: FLIR Tau, FLIR Vue Pro - search & rescue, building diagnostics
Multispectral: MicaSense RedEdge, Sentera 6X - agriculture (NDVI / NDRE indices)
Lidar for inspection: Hesai XT32 or Velodyne Puck Lite - 360° point cloud over towers / pipes

sections.heading: Drone swarms - light shows + autonomous swarms
sections.body: Drone shows (Intel, SkyMagic, High Great) are choreographed swarms - every drone's trajectory is pre-computed centrally and uploaded; the actual flight is just precise mission execution with RTK GPS. Autonomous swarms (Cambridge SkyMul, MIT's Aerial Construction, Saab's Swarmind) make decisions onboard without central coordination - using boids-style local rules, consensus, or learned policies. Defense applications (Loyal Wingman, Replicator, Anduril Roadrunner) drive much of the current state of the art.
bullets: Light shows - centralized choreography, RTK GPS, ~1 cm accuracy required for collision avoidance
Autonomous swarms - onboard decision; local rules (boids), consensus, or learned policies
Comms: 2.4/5 GHz mesh radios (Doodle Labs, Silvus), 900 MHz fallback, low-bandwidth
Coordination: time-synchronized maneuvers via PTP or GPS-disciplined clocks
Crazyswarm - open-source platform for indoor swarm research (Crazyflie + Vicon)
Defense: U.S. DoD Replicator program targets thousands of attritable autonomous systems

sections.heading: Real applications - agriculture, inspection, delivery
sections.body: Drones earn their keep in jobs that would be expensive, dangerous, or impossible from the ground. Precision agriculture (DJI Agras, XAG) sprays + maps fields at 10× the throughput of a tractor. Inspection (Skydio X10, Asio Spire) covers cell towers, wind turbines, bridges without scaffolding. Delivery (Wing, Zipline, Manna, Amazon Prime Air) moves time-critical packages over short distances. Defense + intelligence is the largest market by revenue and growing rapidly with FPV swarms.
bullets: Precision ag - NDVI maps, spot-spray, planting; 1 ha covered in ~10 min vs hours by tractor
Inspection - automated tower / turbine / bridge inspection; 10× faster than rope access
Delivery - Zipline (medical, Africa); Wing (Walmart, Texas); Manna (Ireland)
Cinematography - DJI Inspire / Mavic, autonomous tracking shots
Search + rescue - thermal cameras, NIST Robotics SAR challenges
Defense - Loyal Wingman, Switchblade, Replicator program
Regulation: FAA Part 107 (US) for commercial < 25 kg; BVLOS waivers for autonomous ops
